did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper developed and evaluated DSCC_Net, a deep learning-based skin cancer classification network using convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning. It performed multi-classification of skin cancer types (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanocytic nevi) using dermoscopic images from the ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel deep learning model (DSCC_Net) and presents original experimental results, including performance metrics (accuracy, AUC, recall, precision, F1-score) on specific datasets (ISIC 2020, HAM10000, DermIS), comparing it against baseline models. Although it includes a literature review section, its primary purpose is to report new technical findings and validation of a proposed algorithm.

title: DSCC_Net: Multi-Classification Deep Learning Models for Diagnosing of Skin Cancer Using Dermoscopic Images
brief_summary: This study proposes DSCC_Net, a deep learning-based skin cancer classification network utilizing convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and transfer learning to diagnose four types of skin cancer: melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanocytic nevi. The model was evaluated on three publicly available dermoscopic image datasets: ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS, with SMOTE Tomek employed to address class imbalance. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing DSCC_Net against six baseline deep networks (ResNet-152, Vgg-19, MobileNet, Vgg-16, EfficientNet-B0, and Inception-V3) using metrics such as accuracy, AUC, recall, precision, and F1-score. The proposed DSCC_Net achieved a 94.17% accuracy, 99.43% AUC, 93.76% recall, 94.28% precision, and 93.93% F1-score, outperforming all baseline models. The results indicate that DSCC_Net offers significant support to dermatologists for early and accurate skin cancer diagnosis, although the study notes a limitation regarding its applicability primarily to fair-skinned individuals due to dataset composition.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the four distinct types of skin cancer categorized by the DSCC_Net model. Identified as the most dangerous category that spreads quickly to other organs.
One of the four distinct types of skin cancer categorized by the DSCC_Net model. Described as the most common type of skin cancer, growing slowly in the lower portion of the epidermis.
One of the four distinct types of skin cancer categorized by the DSCC_Net model. Comprises flat cells found in the upper portion of the epidermis, often generated by sun exposure.
One of the four distinct types of skin cancer categorized by the DSCC_Net model. Occurs in pigmented moles, mostly throughout childhood and early adult life.

all_task_types.target: Four distinct types of skin cancer: melanoma (MEL), basal cell carcinoma (BCC), squamous cell carcinoma (SCC), and melanocytic nevi (MN).
all_task_types.details: Multi-classification of skin cancer types using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories for four classes. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, AUC, recall, precision, and F1-score on the ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: The world’s largest collection of dermoscopic images of skin lesions available to the general public, derived from multiple institutes contributing patient data of various ages. The study utilized images from this dataset, specifically focusing on melanoma cases verified by histopathology.

use.training: Used for training the DSCC_Net model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DSCC_Net model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DSCC_Net model.
use.other: The remaining images in the dataset (benign class) were not considered for this research.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A database of 10,015 images produced by the International Skin Image Collaboration in 2018, developed by Queensland University in Australia and the ViDIR Group at the University of Vienna in Austria. It contains seven different data classes identifying skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the DSCC_Net model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DSCC_Net model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DSCC_Net model.
use.other: The rest of the images in the dataset were not considered in this study.

datasets_used.name: DermIS
datasets_used.description: The most comprehensive online dermatology information source offering detailed images, differential diagnoses, and additional information on nearly all skin conditions. It is described as a publicly available benchmark dataset in the study.

use.training: Used for training the DSCC_Net model.
use.validation: Used for validation of the DSCC_Net model.
use.testing: Used for testing the DSCC_Net model.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To handle class imbalance (minority classes) in the dataset before model training and evaluation.

To compare the proposed DSCC_Net model against six baseline deep learning models (ResNet-152, Vgg-19, MobileNet, Vgg-16, EfficientNet-B0, and Inception-V3) using accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, and F1-score.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between DSCC_Net and baseline models were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, F1-score) without statistical significance testing.

ISIC_2020_HAM10000_DermIS_Combined.description: Results aggregated across the three datasets (ISIC 2020, HAM10000, DermIS) using SMOTE Tomek for class imbalance handling. The paper does not provide separate metric breakdowns per dataset in the results section, but reports overall performance.
ISIC_2020_HAM10000_DermIS_Combined.notes: The proposed DSCC_Net with SMOTE Tomek outperformed all baseline models across all metrics. The ROC curve analysis showed DSCC_Net with SMOTE Tomek achieved an AUC of 0.9861, while without SMOTE Tomek it achieved 0.9145. Baseline ROC AUCs ranged from 0.9572 to 0.9818. The study notes that the model's applicability is primarily limited to fair-skinned individuals due to dataset composition.

architecture_used.pretraining: Not reported (baseline models used transfer learning, but DSCC_Net details on pretraining are not explicitly stated; however, it is described as a novel CNN model).

training_details.hardware: Windows 10, 11 GB NVIDIA GPU, 32 GB RAM

preprocessing_techniques: Input image resizing to a fixed resolution of 150 × 150 pixels
Data normalization technique applied to the dataset to prevent overfitting
SMOTE Tomek (Synthetic Minority Oversampling Technique) applied to handle class imbalance and balance the number of samples within each class

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Used to handle class imbalance (minority classes) by generating synthetic samples for underrepresented classes and removing majority samples via Tomek links. Applied to balance the number of samples within each class before model training.

conclusions: The proposed DSCC_Net model, a deep learning-based skin cancer classification network using convolutional neural networks (CNNs), outperformed six baseline models (ResNet-152, Vgg-19, MobileNet, Vgg-16, EfficientNet-B0, and Inception-V3) in multi-classifying four types of skin cancer (melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, squamous cell carcinoma, and melanocytic nevi).

DSCC_Net achieved a 94.17% accuracy, 99.43% AUC, 93.76% recall, 94.28% precision, and 93.93% F1-score when evaluated on combined ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets using SMOTE Tomek for class imbalance handling.
The application of SMOTE Tomek significantly improved the performance of DSCC_Net, increasing accuracy from 83.20% (without SMOTE Tomek) to 94.17% and F1-score from 58.09% to 93.93%.
The study concludes that DSCC_Net offers significant support to dermatologists and health experts for the early and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer.
The study acknowledges a limitation regarding the model's generalization, noting that its applicability is primarily limited to fair-skinned individuals due to the composition of the datasets used.
important_remarks: The study acknowledges a significant limitation regarding population bias: the model's applicability is primarily limited to fair-skinned individuals due to the composition of the ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets.
The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals; performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics without statistical significance testing.
Key training hyperparameters including the optimizer, loss function, learning rate, and batch size were not reported in the text.

The model was evaluated on publicly available benchmark datasets (ISIC 2020, HAM10000, DermIS) rather than an independent external clinical cohort, limiting evidence of generalizability to diverse real-world clinical settings.

Class imbalance was addressed using SMOTE Tomek, which artificially balanced the dataset; while this improved metrics (e.g., F1-score increased from 58.09% to 93.93%), it may not reflect performance on naturally imbalanced clinical data.

The study focuses on multi-classification of four specific skin cancer types (MEL, BCC, SCC, MN) using dermoscopic images, which may not generalize to non-dermoscopic clinical photography or other lesion types.

dataset_usage_details.summary: The study utilized three publicly available dermoscopic image datasets: ISIC 2020, HAM10000 (ISIC 2018), and DermIS. Images from these datasets were pooled together to form a combined dataset for the multi-classification of four skin cancer types: Melanoma (MEL), Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), and Melanocytic Nevi (MN). The combined dataset was split into training, validation, and testing sets. SMOTE Tomek was applied to handle class imbalance. The model was evaluated on this combined internal split; no external or unseen dataset was used for testing.
dataset_usage_details.cross_dataset_validation_details: The paper did not perform cross-dataset validation. The model was trained and tested on a pooled combination of ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets, split internally into training, validation, and testing sets. There is no evidence of training on one dataset and testing on a separate, unseen named dataset.

training.notes: Images were derived from multiple institutes. Only histopathology-verified melanoma images were used; benign images were excluded.
Developed by Queensland University and ViDIR Group. Only specific classes (BCC, MEL, MN) were used; other classes were excluded.

Used to provide images for the Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) class. The total count for DermIS images used is not explicitly stated, but the total SCC count in the combined set is implied by the results (187 SCC images identified correctly + 1 misclassified = 188 total SCC images in the test set, though total training/validation counts are not broken down by source).

validation.notes: Used for model selection/tuning. The paper states the dataset was separated into training, testing, and validation sets, but does not report the specific number of images in the validation set.

testing.counts: Not reported (Total images in test set implied by confusion matrix: 190 BCC + 164 MN + 179 MEL + 188 SCC = 721 images)
testing.notes: Used for final performance evaluation. The results are reported on the combined dataset after applying SMOTE Tomek.

pretraining_or_transfer_learning.notes: The baseline models (ResNet-152, Vgg-19, MobileNet, Vgg-16, EfficientNet-B0, Inception-V3) are standard pre-trained models typically initialized with ImageNet weights. The paper mentions 'transfer learning' in the abstract and keywords, implying the use of pre-trained weights for the baseline models, though specific ImageNet details are not elaborated.

synthetic_or_augmented_data.notes: SMOTE Tomek was used to handle class imbalance by generating synthetic samples for minority classes and removing majority samples. This was applied to the combined dataset before training.

evaluation_protocol.split_details: The paper states the dataset was separated into training, testing, and validation sets. Exact split percentages or fold counts are not reported. The total number of images used from each source is partially reported (579 from ISIC 2020, 3624 from HAM10000, and an unspecified number from DermIS for SCC).
evaluation_protocol.unseen_data_details: No unseen external dataset was used. The testing set was drawn from the same pooled sources as the training and validation sets.

dataset_level_results.notes: Results are aggregated across the combined dataset. The model outperformed all baseline models. Specific per-dataset results are not reported.
Results without SMOTE Tomek show significantly lower performance, particularly in F1-score, due to class imbalance.

generalizability_notes: The study acknowledges a limitation regarding population bias: the model's applicability is primarily limited to fair-skinned individuals due to the composition of the ISIC 2020, HAM10000, and DermIS datasets.
The model was evaluated on publicly available benchmark datasets rather than an independent external clinical cohort, limiting evidence of generalizability to diverse real-world clinical settings.
Class imbalance was addressed using SMOTE Tomek, which artificially balanced the dataset; while this improved metrics, it may not reflect performance on naturally imbalanced clinical data.
information_not_reported: Exact number of images in the training, validation, and testing splits.
Specific number of images from DermIS used for the SCC class.
Whether the split was patient-level or image-level (though image-level is implied by the nature of dermoscopic datasets and lack of patient ID mention).
Specific hyperparameters (optimizer, learning rate, batch size) for the DSCC_Net model.
Formal statistical significance testing (p-values) for the comparison between models.
